1: The superscription places Psalm 59 in the same "Do Not Destroy" (ʾal-tashḥêt) category as Psalms 57-58, with the historical note: Saul sent men to watch David's house to kill him (1 Samuel 19:11-17 — Michal lowered David through a window to escape). הַצִּילֵנִי מֵאֹיְבַי אֱלֹהָי (haṣṣîlênî mêʾōyĕbay ʾĕlōhāy, "Rescue me from my enemies, O my God") — nāṣal (hiphil: to snatch away, to rescue): the urgent rescue from the present danger. מִמִּתְקוֹמְמַי תְּשַׂגְּבֵנִי (mimmitqômĕmay tĕśaggĕbênî, "set me beyond the reach of those who rise up against me") — śāgab (piel: to set on high, to make inaccessibly high): a rock-fortress image — placing the Psalmist on a height beyond the reach of those rising against him.
2: הַצִּילֵנִי מִפֹּעֲלֵי אָוֶן (haṣṣîlênî mippōʿălê ʾāwen, "Rescue me from those who do evil") — pōʿălê ʾāwen: workers of iniquity/trouble (the phrase appears in Psalms 5:5; Psalms 6:8; Psalms 141:9 — an established formula for those whose occupation is harm). וּמֵאַנְשֵׁי דָמִים הוֹשִׁיעֵנִי (ûmêʾanshê dāmîm hôshîʿênî, "save me from men who want my blood") — ʾanshê dāmîm: "men of bloods" — those whose hands are stained with violent bloodshed (the plural dāmîm intensifies: blood upon blood).
3: כִּי הִנֵּה אָרְבוּ לְנַפְשִׁי (kî hinnêh ʾārbû lĕnapshî, "For see — they lie in ambush for my life") — ʾārab: to lie in wait, to ambush (as a predator waits in cover). יָגוּרוּ עָלַי עַזִּים (yāgûrû ʿālay ʿazzîm, "fierce men are gathering against me") — ʿaz: fierce, strong, harsh. לֹא-פִשְׁעִי וְלֹא-חַטָּאתִי יְהוָה (lōʾ-pishʿî wĕlōʾ-ḥaṭṭāʾtî YHWH, "not for any offense or sin of mine, LORD") — the declaration of innocence: not for peshaʿ (rebellious transgression) nor ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin, missing the mark). The two major guilt-terms denied — this is not a deserved persecution.
4: בְּלִי-עָוֹן יְרֻצוּן וְיִכּוֹנָנוּ (bĕlî-ʿāwōn yĕruṣûn wĕyikkônānû, "for no fault of mine they have mobilized against me") — ʿāwōn: the third guilt-term (iniquity/perversity — as in Psalms 32:2), also denied. Rûṣ: to run, to mobilize quickly; kûn (niphal: to prepare, to establish themselves): the enemies are organized and ready. עוּרָה לִקְרָאתִי וּרְאֵה (ʿûrāh liqrāʾtî ûrĕʾêh, "Arouse yourself, come to meet me, and look!") — ʿûr (qal imperative: awake, rouse!) — the same word as Psalms 44:23 ("Wake up! Why are you sleeping?"). The emergency summons to divine attention.
5: וְאַתָּה יְהוָה-אֱלֹהִים צְבָאוֹת (wĕʾattāh YHWH-ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt, "You are the LORD, God of hosts") — the stacking of divine titles: YHWH (the covenant name) + ʾĕlōhîm (God) + ṣĕbāʾôt (armies/hosts) — the full weight of divine identity invoked. אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל הָקִיצָה לִפְקֹד כָּל-הַגּוֹיִם (ʾĕlōhê yisrāʾêl hāqîṣāh lipqōd kol-haggôyim, "God of Israel — rouse yourself to punish all the nations") — pāqad: to attend to, to visit (in judgment): the God who visits the nations with justice. The scope expands: the crisis of one individual becomes a call for universal divine justice over all nations (kol-haggôyim).
6: יָשׁוּבוּ לָעֶרֶב יֶהֱמוּ כַכָּלֶב (yāshûbû lāʿereb yehĕmû kakkāleb, "They return each evening, howling like dogs") — the first occurrence of the distinctive refrain (repeated in v14). Kāleb: dog (not the domestic pet of the modern West but the feral, scavenging pariah dog of the ancient city — dangerous, unclean, unwanted). Hāmāh: to growl, to howl (as in Psalms 46:3 — the roaring waters; Psalms 55:2 — the agitated heart; here: the howling dog-pack). וִיסוֹבְבוּ עִיר (wîsôbĕbû ʿîr, "circling around the city") — the feral dogs circling at night, looking for scraps and opportunity — the enemies who patrol without rest.
7: הִנֵּה יַבִּיעוּן בְּפִיהֶם (hinnêh yabbîʿûn bĕpîhem, "Listen — they pour out venom with their mouths") — nābāʿ (hiphil: to bubble up, to pour out like a fountain): the violent pouring of hostile speech. חֲרָבוֹת בְּשִׂפְתוֹתֵיהֶם (ḥărābôt bĕśiptôtêhem, "their lips are full of swords") — the mouth-as-weapon image (as in Psalms 55:21; Psalms 57:4): the tongue a sword, the lips the sword-case from which blades emerge. כִּי-מִי שֹׁמֵעַ (kî-mî shōmêaʿ, "for they think, 'Who is going to hear?'") — the arrogance of those who believe their scheming is undetected: the assumption of divine absence or inattention.
8: וְאַתָּה יְהוָה תִּשְׂחַק-לָמוֹ (wĕʾattāh YHWH tiśḥaq-lāmô, "But you, LORD, laugh at them") — śāḥaq: to laugh (the divine laughter of Psalms 2:4: "He who sits in the heavens laughs; the LORD holds them in derision"). The contrast: the enemies ask "who hears?" (v7) and the answer is: the LORD — who laughs. תִּלְעַג לְכָל-גּוֹיִם (tilʿag lĕkol-gôyim, "you scoff at all the nations") — lāʿag: to mock, to deride. The divine scoff at the pretensions of the nations who think themselves beyond accountability.
9: עֻזּוֹ אֵלֶיךָ אֶשְׁמֹרָה (ʿuzzô ʾêlêkā ʾeshmōrāh, "O my Strength, I will watch for you") — textual note: the kĕtîb reads ʿuzzô ("his strength") and the qĕrê reads ʿuzzî ("my strength"). Either way, the Psalmist watches (shāmar: to keep, to guard — or here: to wait watchfully) for the divine strength to appear. כִּי-אֱלֹהִים מִשְׂגַּבִּי (kî-ʾĕlōhîm miśgabbî, "for God is my fortress") — miśgāb: the high fortress (as in Psalms 46:7 — the YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt refrain: "the God of Jacob is our fortress").
10: אֱלֹהֵי חַסְדִּי יְקַדְּמֵנִי (ʾĕlōhê ḥasdî yĕqaddĕmênî, "The God of my steadfast love will come ahead of me") — qādam (piel: to go before, to come ahead of, to meet in advance): God will arrive before the Psalmist needs him — the divine advance-party. Ĥesed as God's defining character — "God of my ḥesed" personalizes the covenant: the covenant-loyalty is owned as the Psalmist's own possession (as in Psalms 23:6). אֱלֹהִים יַרְאֵנִי בְשֹׁרְרָי (ʾĕlōhîm yarʾênî bĕshōrĕray, "God will let me look in triumph on my enemies") — rāʾāh: to see; shōrēr: an adversary (watching with hostile intent). God showing the Psalmist the sight of the enemy's defeat — the vindication made visible.
11: אַל-תַּהַרְגֵם פֶּן-יִשְׁכְּחוּ עַמִּי (ʾal-tahargêm pen-yishkĕḥû ʿammî, "Do not kill them, lest my people forget") — the most theologically remarkable verse in the psalm: the prayer for restraint. The enemies are not to be destroyed outright but kept as a living demonstration of divine justice. Yishkĕḥû: "they forget" — the people would forget the lesson if the enemies disappeared too quickly. The defeated enemy as a living monument to divine judgment, a pedagogical retention for the sake of communal memory. הֲנִיעֵמוֹ בְחֵילְךָ וְהוֹרִידֵמוֹ (hănîʿêmô bĕḥêlĕkā wĕhôrîdêmô, "bring them down by your power and humble them") — nûaʿ (hiphil: to make to totter, to shake); yārad (hiphil: to bring low). Shaken and brought low — diminished but not eliminated.
12: חַטַּאת-פִּימוֹ דְּבַר-שְׂפָתֵימוֹ (ḥaṭṭat-pîmô dĕbar-śĕpātêmô, "For the sin of their mouths and the words of their lips") — the sin is localized: the mouth, the lips, the speech. וְיִלָּכְדוּ בִגְאוֹנָם (wĕyillākĕdû bigĕʾônām, "let them be ensnared in their own pride") — lāḵad (niphal: to be caught, trapped — in a net or trap): the ironic justice of the trap. Gaʾăwāh: pride, arrogance (the swelling up of self). Pride as the snare: the arrogant fall into the trap of their own overconfidence.
13: כַּלֵּה בְחֵמָה כַּלֵּה וְאֵינֵמוֹ (kallêh bĕḥêmāh kallêh wĕʾênêmô, "Consume them in your wrath; consume them until they are no more") — kālāh (piel: to bring to completion, to finish — the word used of God finishing the work of creation in Genesis 2:1-2): bring them to a complete end. Ḥêmāh: divine rage (the heated wrath). וְיֵדְעוּ כִּי-אֱלֹהִים מֹשֵׁל בְּיַעֲקֹב לְאַפְסֵי הָאָרֶץ (wĕyêdĕʿû kî-ʾĕlōhîm mōshêl bĕyaʿăqōb lĕʾapsê hāʾāreṣ, "so that people may know that God rules over Jacob to the ends of the earth") — the goal of the judgment: universal knowledge (yādaʿ) that God reigns over Jacob — and through Jacob, to the ends of the earth (ʾapsê hāʾāreṣ: the furthest limits). The individual's deliverance has universal theological purpose.
14: The refrain appears again (identical to v6): יָשׁוּבוּ לָעֶרֶב יֶהֱמוּ כַכָּלֶב וִיסוֹבְבוּ עִיר — "They return each evening, howling like dogs and circling the city." The structural repetition of the refrain within the psalm creates a two-stanza hymn, each stanza moving from the image of the feral dogs (vv6/14) to the Psalmist's trust and the divine response. The dogs' circling is relentless — but the divine response (vv7-13, vv15-17) frames it on both sides.
15: הֵמָּה יְנִיעוּן לֶאֱכֹל (hêmmāh yĕnîʿûn leʾĕkōl, "They roam around in search of food") — nûaʿ: to wander, to shake from side to side (the hungry dog's restless search). The hungry scavenger as the image of the aimless, unsatisfied enemy. אִם-לֹא יִשְׂבְּעוּ וַיָּלִינוּ (ʾim-lōʾ yiśbĕʿû wayyālînû, "they growl when they are not satisfied") — lûn: to pass the night, to spend the night (the dogs not going home — staying out through the dark hours). The unsatisfied growl of the unfed predator who cannot catch what it hunts.
16: וַאֲנִי אָשִׁיר עֻזֶּךָ (waʾănî ʾāshîr ʿuzzekā, "But I will sing of your strength") — the emphatic waʾănî (but I!) contrasting the howling dogs with the Psalmist's song. ʿŌz: strength. וַאֲרַנֵּן לַבֹּקֶר חַסְדֶּךָ (waʾărannên labbōqer ḥasdekā, "I will sing aloud of your steadfast love in the morning") — rānan: to shout for joy (the ringing joyful cry). Labbōqer: in the morning (the dogs prowl through the dark night; the Psalmist greets the morning with praise). כִּי-הָיִיתָ מִשְׂגָּב לִי (kî-hāyîtā miśgāb lî, "for you have been my fortress") — miśgāb: the high fortress of Psalms 46:7/11 — "the God of Jacob is our miśgāb."
17: עֻזִּי אֵלֶיךָ אֲזַמֵּרָה (ʿuzzî ʾêlêkā ʾăzammêrāh, "O my Strength, I will sing praise to you") — the closing verse mirrors v9: the ʿōz (strength) that was watched for is now the strength to which praise is directed. כִּי-אֱלֹהִים מִשְׂגַּבִּי (kî-ʾĕlōhîm miśgabbî, "for God is my fortress") — the identical phrase from v9, closing the psalm with the same confession. אֱלֹהֵי חַסְדִּי (ʾĕlōhê ḥasdî, "the God who shows me steadfast love") — ḥesed as the final word of the psalm: the covenant loyalty that grounds the strength, the fortress, and the morning song. The dogs howl into the night; the Psalmist praises into the morning.
